---
title: "Day 4 — Exercise: Plot a read-length histogram with `plotters`"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Given a vector of read lengths, produce a histogram as a PNG file using the [`plotters`](https://docs.rs/plotters/) crate:

```rust
fn plot_histogram(lengths: &[usize], bin_width: usize, output_path: &Path) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>>
```

This is your first plot in Rust. `plotters` is an imperative builder API (compared to ggplot2's grammar of graphics), but the conceptual pieces are the same: a drawing surface, axes with scales, a chart, and one or more data series.

## The crate

```toml
[dependencies]
plotters = "0.3"
```

`plotters` ships a `prelude` module that brings most of what you need into scope with one `use`:

```rust
use plotters::prelude::*;
```

You will see:

- [`BitMapBackend`](https://docs.rs/plotters/latest/plotters/backend/struct.BitMapBackend.html) — the PNG drawing backend (also available: [`SVGBackend`](https://docs.rs/plotters/latest/plotters/backend/struct.SVGBackend.html))
- [`DrawingArea`](https://docs.rs/plotters/latest/plotters/drawing/struct.DrawingArea.html) — the canvas
- [`ChartBuilder`](https://docs.rs/plotters/latest/plotters/chart/struct.ChartBuilder.html) — configures axes, captions, margins
- [`Rectangle`](https://docs.rs/plotters/latest/plotters/element/struct.Rectangle.html) — what you draw for each histogram bar

## The exercise

```bash
cd day4/ex-plot-readlen
```

Make the two tests pass — they call your function with synthetic data and check that (a) it produces a non-empty PNG file at the requested path, (b) the PNG starts with the standard PNG magic bytes.

## How to run

```bash
cargo test
cargo run -- 5 readlen.png    # synthetic data, bin width 5, output readlen.png
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — bin the data"}
Group the lengths into bins of the given width. The histogram bar at position `b` covers lengths `b * bin_width..(b+1) * bin_width`:

```rust
let max = *lengths.iter().max().unwrap_or(&0);
let n_bins = max / bin_width + 1;
let mut counts: Vec<u32> = vec![0; n_bins];
for &l in lengths {
    counts[l / bin_width] += 1;
}
let max_count = *counts.iter().max().unwrap_or(&0);
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — set up the drawing area and chart"}
```rust
let root = BitMapBackend::new(output_path, (800, 600)).into_drawing_area();
root.fill(&WHITE)?;
let mut chart = ChartBuilder::on(&root)
    .caption("Read length distribution", ("sans-serif", 30))
    .margin(20)
    .x_label_area_size(40)
    .y_label_area_size(50)
    .build_cartesian_2d(0u32..(n_bins as u32 * bin_width as u32), 0u32..(max_count + 1))?;
chart.configure_mesh()
    .x_desc("read length")
    .y_desc("count")
    .draw()?;
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — draw a bar per bin"}
Each bar is a [`Rectangle`](https://docs.rs/plotters/latest/plotters/element/struct.Rectangle.html) from `(b * bin_width, 0)` to `((b+1) * bin_width, count)`:

```rust
chart.draw_series(counts.iter().enumerate().map(|(b, &c)| {
    let x0 = (b * bin_width) as u32;
    let x1 = ((b + 1) * bin_width) as u32;
    Rectangle::new([(x0, 0u32), (x1, c)], BLUE.mix(0.6).filled())
}))?;
root.present()?;
```

[`BLUE.mix(0.6)`](https://docs.rs/plotters/latest/plotters/style/colors/index.html) gives a slightly translucent blue; `.filled()` fills the rectangle rather than just outlining it.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
See `day4/ex-plot-readlen-solution/src/main.rs` for the full reference. The whole function is about 25 lines.
:::

## Where this leads

- Swap `BitMapBackend` for `SVGBackend` and the output is SVG with no other changes.
- The [`plotters::series::Histogram`](https://docs.rs/plotters/latest/plotters/series/struct.Histogram.html) type can replace the `draw_series(... Rectangle ...)` loop, but it requires the same kinds of inputs and is not significantly shorter for this case.
- Read FASTQ records with [`noodles-fastq`](https://docs.rs/noodles-fastq/), collect their lengths into a `Vec<usize>`, and feed the result into `plot_histogram`. That is the production version of this exercise.
